channel.title: 10. Logical Flaw Questions on Arnesh's Website
channel.description: Recent content in 10. Logical Flaw Questions on Arnesh's Website

item.title: 10.1 Introduction
item.description: Lesson Content In our review of GMAT Critical Reasoning questions, we have seen many arguments that are flawed. Logical Flaw questions also present us with stimuli containing flawed arguments, but the focus of Logical Flaw questions is somewhat different from the foci of the other types of questions, in that Logical Flaw questions are more directly focused on the flaws in the arguments. choose an answer that best describes a flaw found in the argument (in the case of a pure Logical Flaw question).

item.title: 10.1.1 The Connection Between Assumptions and Logical Flaws
item.description: Lesson Content Remember – an assumption is a piece of missing information that must be true for the logic of an argument to be sound. 💡MUST KNOW: An assumption is a piece of missing information that must be true for the support for an argument to be sound. As you know well by this point, authors make assumptions when making arguments. Let’s look at a few examples: Health insurance for those living in major cities costs significantly more than does health insurance for those living in small towns.

item.title: 10.2 Common Logical Flaw Question Stems
item.description: Lesson Content As you already know, Critical Reasoning question stems are predictable, and the more quickly we can determine what we have to do in order to correctly answer the question we’re facing, the more time we’ll have for answering it. The following are common ways in which Logical Flaw Questions are worded: Which of the following best describes the error of reasoning contained in the argument above? Which of the following best describes a weakness present in the argument above?

item.title: 10.2.1 Pure Logical Flaw Questions
item.description: Lesson Content The more basic type of Logical Flaw question is a pure Logical Flaw question. 💡MUST KNOW: Some Logical Flaw Questions are pure Logical Flaw questions, meaning that they ask us to point out a flaw in the argument as it currently stands and without bringing in new information in the correct answer. Let’s look at an example stimulus of a pure Logical Flaw question: In a recent medical study, all of the study participants who had in the past five years abused barbiturates, powerful prescription sedatives commonly used to treat anxiety, were found to be suffering with depression.

item.title: 10.2.2 Reviewing the Difference Between a Logical Flaw Question and a Weaken the Argument Question
item.description: Lesson Content Logical Flaw questions may seem to be rather similar to Weaken the Argument questions. As we’ve discussed, almost always, in order to weaken the conclusion in a Weaken the Argument question, the correct answer option brings in new, outside information. On the other hand, in answering a pure Logical Flaw question, we are not seeking to weaken the argument. 💡MUST KNOW: In answering a pure Logical Flaw question, we are not seeking to weaken the argument.

item.title: 10.2.3 Logical Flaw Questions Hybridized with Weaken the Argument Questions
item.description: Lesson Content The second Logical Flaw question type contains questions that are hybrids of Weaken the Argument questions and pure Logical Flaw questions. 💡MUST KNOW: Some Logical Flaw Questions are hybrids of Weaken the Argument questions and pure Logical Flaw questions. These questions also ask us to point out a flaw in the author’s argument, but they bring in new information in the correct answer, in a way quite similar to the way in which regular Weaken the Argument questions do.

item.title: 10.2.4 Common Flaws in Reasoning Found in Logical Flaw Questions
item.description: Lesson Content There are several mistakes (flaws) in reasoning that authors commonly make in their arguments. Errors in Reasoning Involving Cause and Effect Overgeneralizing and Making Sampling Errors Confusion Related to Numbers, Percentages or Other Numerical Information Conclusions that are not Supported by the Evidence Confusing Necessary or Significant Information with Sufficient Information The good news is that any of these concepts that are fair game in Weaken the Argument questions are also fair game in Logical Flaw questions.

item.title: 10.2.5 Logical Flaw One: Errors in Reasoning Involving Cause and Effect
item.description: Lesson Content Authors of arguments often misconstrue or misperceive the nature of cause-and-effect relationships. In Logical Flaw questions, we often see authors and speakers making mistakes associated with cause-and-effect reasoning. The most common mistakes center on the following three flaws in reasoning: Here’s an error in cause-and-effect reasoning in the context of a Logical Flaw question: The transportation secretary of the city of Niezque noticed that, in areas of the city in which there were many bicycle repair shops, there tended to be great numbers of people using bicycles.

item.title: 10.2.6 Logical Flaw Two: Overgeneralization and Sampling Flaws
item.description: Lesson Content As we have already discussed in another chapter, when making a generalization, we base a broad claim about a larger population upon observations of a small sample of a population. For example, let’s say that we know ten people who live in Dallas, and all vote Republican. In answering Logical Flaw questions, be on the lookout for broad generalizations made from small samples; such generalizations will be suspect.

item.title: 10.2.7 Logical Flaw Three: Confusion Related to Numbers, Percentages, or Other Numerical Information
item.description: Lesson Content As has been discussed in other chapters, authors commonly make errors in reasoning based on numbers and percentages. Consider the following example: During the past 25 years, the percentage of Greysoft employees who invest their retirement monies in the company mutual fund has decreased by fifteen percent. Since a certain number of investors withdraw their money from the company mutual fund each year, to function optimally, the mutual fund requires a consistent number of new investors.

item.title: 10.2.8 Logical Flaw Four: Conclusions that are Not Supported by the Evidence
item.description: Lesson Content Sometimes an author will draw a conclusion that’s simply not supported by the evidence provided. For example, an author could seek to support the conclusion that a product is safe to use by going on about all the testing of the product that has been done. Notice how the evidence in the following example doesn’t really support the conclusion. Professor: Over the past few years, I have noticed that the students coming to our school are less motivated than students here were in previous years.

item.title: 10.2.9 Logical Flaw Five: Confusing Necessary or Significant Information with Sufficient Information
item.description: Lesson Content Necessary information, significant information, and sufficient information differ from each other in key ways. 💡MUST KNOW: Necessary information, significant information, and sufficient information differ from each other in key ways. At the same time, since they are somewhat similar, these types of information can be misused in arguments, in which cases the arguments are flawed. A key thing to understand about these types of information is that information that is necessary or significant is not necessarily the same as information that is sufficient for supporting a conclusion.

item.title: 10.3 Types of Incorrect Answers in Logical Flaw Questions
item.description: Lesson Content A key aspect of choosing correct answers to Logical Flaw questions is, of course, not getting tempted into choosing answer choices that are correct sounding but incorrect. Let’s go through some types of Logical Flaw incorrect answers to better prepare you to see them for what they are.

item.title: 10.3.1 Incorrect Choices that Describe Things that the Arguments Do Not Actually Do
item.description: Lesson Content The most obviously wrong answer choices in Logical Flaw questions are those that describe things that authors or arguments don’t actually do. Consider the following example: Unlike other major automobile companies, all of which produce mostly automobiles that are fossil fuel powered, The Blitz Automobile Company makes only electric automobiles. Because electric automobiles are easier to maintain, less costly to power, and more environmentally friendly, they are superior to fossil fuel powered vehicles.

item.title: 10.3.2 Incorrect Choices that Bring Up Things that the Argument Does but are not Flaws
item.description: Lesson Content In order for a Logical Flaw answer choice to be correct, it has to have two characteristics. 💡MUST KNOW: In order for a Logical Flaw answer choice to be correct, it has to have two characteristics. It has to describe something that actually goes on in the argument, and what it describes has to be a flaw in the argument. Many Logical Flaw incorrect answer choices satisfy the first criterion, as they describe things that the authors or arguments actually do, but do not satisfy the second one, as what they describe are not flaws.

item.title: 10.3.3 Incorrect Choices that Describe Flaws in the Support for the Wrong Conclusions
item.description: Lesson Content As we have seen in reviewing various types of Critical Reasoning questions, one way in which the creator of a question can create a tempting wrong answer choice is to have the choice address a conclusion that is closely related to the conclusion of the argument but is not actually the conclusion of the argument. 💡MUST KNOW: One way to create a tempting wrong answer choice is to have the choice address a conclusion that is closely related to the conclusion of the argument but is not actually the conclusion of the argument.

item.title: 10.3.4 Incorrect Choices that Bring Up Mathematical Information that Doesn’t Matter
item.description: Lesson Content The argument in a Logical Flaw question can have a conclusion that is math related, such as a conclusion based on population data, a conclusion based on research results, or a conclusion about company profits. 💡MUST KNOW: Logical Flaw questions involving mathematical arguments present an opportunity for a question writer to create answer choices that are tempting but incorrect because they bring up mathematical information that doesn’t matter for the support of the conclusion presented.
